#!/bin/bash

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! rpm -q aide &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y aide
fi

# Generate and initialize the AIDE database if it doesn't exist
AIDE_DB="/var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz"
if [ ! -f "$AIDE_DB" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "$AIDE_DB")"
    aide --init
    mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz "$AIDE_DB"
fi

# Ensure AIDE is configured to use the generated database
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide.conf"
if grep -q "^database=file:" "$AIDE_CONF"; then
    sed -i "s|^database=.*|database=file:$AIDE_DB|" "$AIDE_CONF"
else
    echo "database=file:$AIDE_DB" >> "$AIDE_CONF"
fi

# Ensure AIDE cron job is present for regular checks
CRON_JOB="@daily /usr/sbin/aide --check > /var/log/aide.log 2>&1"
if ! crontab -l | grep -q "$CRON_JOB"; then
    (crontab -l; echo "$CRON_JOB") | crontab -
fi

# Ensure AIDE is enabled and running
systemctl enable aidecheck.service
systemctl start aidecheck.service

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit tools to be monitored by AIDE
audit_tools=(
    /usr/bin/auditctl
    /usr/sbin/aureport
    /usr/sbin/ausearch
    /usr/sbin/autrace
    /usr/sbin/audispd
)

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! command -v aide &> /dev/null; then
    echo "AIDE is not installed. Installing..."
    sudo yum install -y aide
fi

# Backup the original AIDE configuration file
sudo cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak

# Function to add or update entries in AIDE configuration
update_aide_config() {
    local tool_path="$1"
    if grep -q "^$tool_path" /etc/aide.conf; then
        echo "Entry for $tool_path already exists. Updating..."
        sudo sed -i "/^$tool_path/c\\$tool_path = R, p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+t" /etc/aide.conf
    else
        echo "Adding entry for $tool_path..."
        echo "$tool_path = R, p+i+n+u+g+s+b+m+c+t" | sudo tee -a /etc/aide.conf > /dev/null
    fi
}

# Update AIDE configuration for each audit tool
for tool in "${audit_tools[@]}"; do
    update_aide_config "$tool"
done

# Initialize AIDE database with the updated configuration
sudo aide --init

# Move the new database to the default location
sudo mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz

# Ensure AIDE cron job is enabled for regular checks
if ! crontab -l | grep -q "aide --check"; then
    echo "Adding AIDE cron job..."
    (crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo "0 5 * * * /usr/sbin/aide --check") | crontab -
fi

echo "AIDE configuration updated to monitor audit tools."

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! command -v aide &> /dev/null; then
    echo "AIDE is not installed. Please install it first."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original AIDE configuration file
CONFIG_FILE="/etc/aide.conf"
BACKUP_FILE="${CONFIG_FILE}.bak"

if [ ! -f "$BACKUP_FILE" ]; then
    cp "$CONFIG_FILE" "$BACKUP_FILE"
fi

# Function to enable extended attribute checking in a ruleset
enable_xattrs() {
    local RULESET="$1"
    sed -i "/^$RULESET/ s/\(.*\)/\1+xattrs/" "$CONFIG_FILE"
}

# Define the default rulesets to check
DEFAULT_RULESETS=("ALL" "NORMAL" "DATA")

# Enable extended attribute checking for each defined ruleset
for RULESET in "${DEFAULT_RULESETS[@]}"; do
    if grep -q "^$RULESET\b" "$CONFIG_FILE"; then
        enable_xattrs "$RULESET"
    fi
done

# Reload AIDE to apply changes
aide --init
systemctl restart aide.service

echo "Extended attribute checking has been enabled for all defined rulesets in AIDE."

if ! fips-mode-setup --enable; then

# Verify GRUB configuration includes fips=1

    echo "GRUB configuration does not include fips=1"

fi

# Verify that the kernel command line includes the crypto policy setting

    echo "Failed to set the default cryptographic policy in the kernel command line."
    exit 1
fi

GNUTLS_CONF="/etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/gnutls.config"

# Set the desired cryptographic policy
echo "SYSTEM_PRIORITY = \"FIPS140-2\";" > "$GNUTLS_CONF"

# Disable legacy/insecure protocol versions
echo "DISABLED_PROTOCOLS = SSL3, TLS1_0, TLS1_1, DTLS0_9, DTLS1_0;" >> "$GNUTLS_CONF"

# Ensure the configuration file is owned by root and has the correct permissions

systemctl restart gnutls-serv.service gnutls-cli.service

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure GPG signature verification is enabled for local package installations

# Check if the gpgcheck option is set to 1 in /etc/yum.conf
if ! grep -q "^gpgcheck=1" /etc/yum.conf; then
    # Add or update the gpgcheck option to 1 in /etc/yum.conf
    sed -i 's/^gpgcheck=.*/gpgcheck=1/' /etc/yum.conf
fi

# Check if the localrepo section exists and gpgcheck is set to 1 in /etc/yum.repos.d/local.repo
if [ -f /etc/yum.repos.d/local.repo ]; then
    if ! grep -q "^\[localrepo\]" /etc/yum.repos.d/local.repo; then
        echo "[localrepo]" | sudo tee -a /etc/yum.repos.d/local.repo > /dev/null
    fi
    if ! grep -q "^gpgcheck=1" /etc/yum.repos.d/local.repo; then
        sed -i '/^\[localrepo\]/a gpgcheck=1' /etc/yum.repos.d/local.repo

    # Add the banner display directive to the SSH configuration
    echo "Banner /etc/motd" >> "$sshd_config"
fi

# Restart the SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Define the approved organizational warning/consent banner text
BANNER_TEXT="AUTHORIZED ACCESS ONLY. Unauthorized access is strictly prohibited."

# Set the GNOME Display Manager login screen banner
gsettings set org.gnome.login-screen banner-message-enable true
gsettings set org.gnome.login-screen banner-message-text "$BANNER_TEXT"

# Lock the settings so non-privileged users cannot override them
dconf write /org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-enable "true"
dconf write /org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-text "'$BANNER_TEXT'"

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots by creating a dconf profile
PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"
mkdir -p "$PROFILE_DIR"
echo "user-db:user" > "$PROFILE_DIR/gdm"

# Create or update the user database with the banner settings
DB_PATH="/etc/dconf/db/user.d/01-banner-settings"
cat <<EOF > "$DB_PATH"
[org/gnome/login-screen]
banner-message-enable=true
banner-message-text='$BANNER_TEXT'
EOF

# Update the dconf database to apply changes
dconf update

# Verify that the settings are correctly applied and locked
CURRENT_BANNER_TEXT=$(gsettings get org.gnome.login-screen banner-message-text)
if [ "$CURRENT_BANNER_TEXT" == "'$BANNER_TEXT'" ]; then
    echo "Banner text set and locked successfully."
else
    echo "Failed to set or lock banner text."
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define the desired time window for failed login attempts
TIME_WINDOW=900

# Check if the pam_tally2 module is already configured with the desired time window
if grep -q "pam_tally2.so.*deny=" /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    # Extract the current deny value
    CURRENT_DENY=$(grep -oP "(?<=deny=)\d+" /etc/pam.d/system-auth)
    
    # Check if the deny value is already set to a value that implies the desired time window
    if [ "$CURRENT_DENY" -ge 3 ]; then
        echo "pam_tally2 is already configured with a sufficient deny value."
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Modify the /etc/pam.d/system-auth file to set the deny value and reset_time option
sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_tally2.so/s/$/ deny=3 unlock_time='$TIME_WINDOW'/' /etc/pam.d/system-auth

# Ensure that the changes persist across reboots by adding a line to /etc/security/faillock.conf
if ! grep -q "unlock_time = $TIME_WINDOW" /etc/security/faillock.conf; then
    echo "unlock_time = $TIME_WINDOW" >> /etc/security/faillock.conf
fi

# Restart the sshd service to apply the changes
systemctl restart sshd

echo "Failed login tracking interval set to $TIME_WINDOW seconds."

# Ensure PAM configuration for lockout mechanism suppresses feedback

# Define the PAM configuration file path
PAM_CONF="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"

# Check if the required line is already present in the PAM configuration
if ! grep -q "^auth\s*required\s*pam_tally2.so\s*onerr=fail\s*deny=3\s*nologin" "$PAM_CONF"; then
    # Add the line to suppress feedback on failed login attempts
    sed -i '/^auth\s*required\s*pam_unix.so/a auth required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail deny=3 nologin' "$PAM_CONF"
fi

# Ensure the change persists across reboots by checking and setting SELinux context if necessary
if ! restorecon -v "$PAM_CONF" &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Failed to set SELinux context for $PAM_CONF. Please check SELinux policies."
fi

# Apply the same configuration to other relevant authentication stacks
for auth_stack in common-auth common-password common-session; do
    AUTH_STACK_FILE="/etc/pam.d/$auth_stack"
    if ! grep -q "^auth\s*required\s*pam_tally2.so\s*onerr=fail\s*deny=3\s*nologin" "$AUTH_STACK_FILE"; then
        sed -i '/^auth\s*required\s*pam_unix.so/a auth required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail deny=3 nologin' "$AUTH_STACK_FILE"
    fi
    if ! restorecon -v "$AUTH_STACK_FILE" &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Failed to set SELinux context for $AUTH_STACK_FILE. Please check SELinux policies."
    fi
done

# Restart the SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the pam_pwquality module is installed
if ! rpm -q pam_pwquality; then
    yum install -y pam_pwquality
fi

# Configure PAM to enforce dictionary word checking
pam_config="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
backup_file="${pam_config}.bak"

# Create a backup if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "$backup_file" ]; then
    cp "$pam_config" "$backup_file"
fi

# Ensure the 'dictcheck' option is set to 1 in pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^dictcheck/s/=.*/=1/' "$pam_config"

# Ensure the 'enforce_for_root' option is set to 1 in pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^enforce_for_root/s/=.*/=1/' "$pam_config"

# Ensure the PAM configuration includes pam_pwquality.so
auth_file="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
password_file="/etc/pam.d/password-auth"

for file in "$auth_file" "$password_file"; do
    if ! grep -q 'pam_pwquality.so' "$file"; then
        sed -i '/^password.*requisite.*pam_cracklib\.so/a password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only retry=3 authtok_type=' "$file"
    fi
done

# Ensure the changes are applied by reloading PAM services
systemctl restart systemd-logind.service

# Configure PAM to enforce dictionary word checking

if [ ! -f "$backup_file" ]; then
    cp "$pam_config" "$backup_file"

sed -i '/^dictcheck/s/=.*/=1/' "$pam_config"

sed -i '/^enforce_for_root/s/=.*/=1/' "$pam_config"

# Ensure the PAM configuration includes pam_pwquality.so
auth_file="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"

#!/bin/bash

# Define the path to the PAM configuration file for password changes
PAM_CONF="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"

# Define the required minimum different characters
MIN_DIFF_CHARS=8

# Function to check if the PAM module is already configured with the required option
is_configured() {
    grep -q "password.*pam_cracklib.so.*difok=$MIN_DIFF_CHARS" "$PAM_CONF"
}

# If the configuration is not present, add it
if ! is_configured; then
    # Backup the original PAM configuration file
    cp "$PAM_CONF" "$PAM_CONF.bak"

    # Add the required option to the PAM configuration file
    sed -i "/password.*pam_cracklib.so/s/$/ difok=$MIN_DIFF_CHARS/" "$PAM_CONF"
fi

# Ensure that the changes persist across reboots by reloading the systemd manager configuration
systemctl daemon-reload

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum number of days after password expiration when an account should be disabled
MAX_INACTIVE_DAYS=30

# Update the default useradd configuration to set the inactivity period
echo "INACTIVE=${MAX_INACTIVE_DAYS}" | sudo tee -a /etc/default/useradd > /dev/null

# Ensure the change is persistent across reboots by checking if the line already exists and updating it if necessary
if ! grep -q "^INACTIVE=" /etc/default/useradd; then
    echo "INACTIVE=${MAX_INACTIVE_DAYS}" | sudo tee -a /etc/default/useradd > /dev/null
else
    sudo sed -i "s/^INACTIVE=.*/INACTIVE=${MAX_INACTIVE_DAYS}/" /etc/default/useradd
fi

# Apply the change to existing user accounts by setting their inactivity period
for user in $(awk -F: '$7 != "" {print $1}' /etc/passwd); do
    current_inactive=$(chage -l "$user" | grep "Account expires" | awk '{print $4}')
    if [[ "$current_inactive" == "never" ]]; then
        sudo chage -I ${MAX_INACTIVE_DAYS} "$user"
    fi
done

# Verify the configuration by checking a sample user's inactivity period
sample_user=$(awk -F: '$7 != "" {print $1; exit}' /etc/passwd)
if [[ $(chage -l "$sample_user" | grep "Account expires" | awk '{print $4}') -eq ${MAX_INACTIVE_DAYS} ]]; then
    echo "Configuration successful for user $sample_user"
else
    echo "Configuration failed for user $sample_user"
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define the target permissions
TARGET_PERMISSIONS="0740"

# Function to apply permissions to files
apply_permissions() {
    local file="$1"
    local current_permissions
    current_permissions=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
    
    # Only change permissions if they are more permissive than the target
    if (( 10#$current_permissions > 10#$TARGET_PERMISSIONS )); then
        chmod "$TARGET_PERMISSIONS" "$file"
    fi
}

# Iterate over all users with home directories and root
for user in $(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd) root; do
    home_dir=$(getent passwd "$user" | cut -d: -f6)
    
    # Check if the home directory exists
    if [[ -d "$home_dir" ]]; then
        # Apply permissions to all dot-files in the home directory
        for file in "$home_dir"/.[^.]*; do
            if [[ -e "$file" && ! -L "$file" ]]; then
                apply_permissions "$file"
            fi
        done
    fi
done

# Function to enable Kernel Page-Table Isolation (KPTI) in GRUB configuration
enable_kpti() {
    local grub_cfg="/etc/default/grub"
    
    # Check if the KPTI parameter is already set
    if grep -q "^\s*GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$grub_cfg"; then

    grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
}

# Apply KPTI to all currently installed kernels and future kernels
enable_kpti

}

echo "kernel.vsyscall = off" | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-vsyscall.conf > /dev/null

# Apply the setting immediately
sudo sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-vsyscall.conf

# Ensure the setting is applied to all currently installed kernels and future kernels

    sudo grubby --update-kernel=/boot/vmlinuz-$kernel --args="vsyscall=none"

if [[ $(grubby --info DEFAULT | grep -oP '(?<=vsyscall=)\w+') == "none" ]]; then
    echo "Successfully disabled legacy virtual syscall mechanism."
else
    echo "Failed to disable legacy virtual syscall mechanism."
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Function to disable IPv6 router advertisements on all interfaces
disable_ipv6_ra() {
    # Iterate over all network interfaces
    for interface in /sys/class/net/*; do
        interface_name=$(basename "$interface")
        
        # Check if the interface supports IPv6
        if [ -d "/proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/$interface_name" ]; then
            # Disable router advertisements at runtime
            echo 0 > "/proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/$interface_name/accept_ra"
            
            # Ensure the change persists across reboots by updating sysctl configuration
            if ! grep -q "net.ipv6.conf.$interface_name.accept_ra = 0" /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-ra.conf; then
                echo "net.ipv6.conf.$interface_name.accept_ra = 0" >> /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-ra.conf
            fi
        fi
    done
    
    # Apply the sysctl changes immediately
    sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-ra.conf
}

# Execute the function to disable IPv6 router advertisements
disable_ipv6_ra

#!/bin/bash

# Function to apply noexec,nosuid,nodev options to non-root local filesystems
apply_security_options() {
    # Get all mounted filesystems that are not root and are local
    local mounts=$(mount | awk '$3 != "/" && $1 ~ /^[^ ]+$/ && $2 !~ /^\/proc|^\/sys|^\/dev/ {print $3}')

    for mount_point in $mounts; do
        # Check if the filesystem already has noexec,nosuid,nodev options
        current_options=$(grep -E "^$mount_point" /etc/fstab | awk '{print $4}')
        
        if [[ ! "$current_options" =~ "noexec" || ! "$current_options" =~ "nosuid" || ! "$current_options" =~ "nodev" ]]; then
            # Add the missing options to the current options
            new_options="${current_options},noexec,nosuid,nodev"
            
            # Update /etc/fstab with the new options
            sed -i.bak "/^$mount_point/s/^\([^#]*\)\(.*\)$/\1 $new_options \2/" /etc/fstab
            
            # Remount the filesystem with the new options
            mount -o remount,noexec,nosuid,nodev "$mount_point"
        fi
    done
}

# Apply security options
apply_security_options

        # Check if the filesystem already has noexec,nosuid,nodev options
        current_options=$(grep -E "^$mount_point" /etc/fstab | awk '{print $4}')
        
        if [[ ! "$current_options" =~ "noexec" || ! "$current_options" =~ "nosuid" || ! "$current_options" =~ "nodev" ]]; then
            # Add the missing options to the current options
            new_options="${current_options},noexec,nosuid,nodev"
            
            # Update /etc/fstab with the new options
            sed -i.bak "/^$mount_point/s/^\([^#]*\)\(.*\)$/\1 $new_options \2/" /etc/fstab
            
            # Remount the filesystem with the new options
            mount -o remount,noexec,nosuid,nodev "$mount_point"
        fi
    done
}

# Apply security options
apply_security_options
```

# Define the kernel parameter to enable free-page poisoning
KERNEL_PARAM="page_poison=1"

# Function to update GRUB configuration for all installed kernels
update_grub_for_all_kernels() {
    local grub_cfg="/etc/default/grub"
    
    # Check if the parameter is already set in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
    if ! grep -qE "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX.*$KERNEL_PARAM" "$grub_cfg"; then
        # Append the parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
        sed -i "/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=/ s/\"$/ $KERNEL_PARAM\"/" "$grub_cfg"
    fi

    # Update GRUB configuration
    grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
}

# Function to ensure the parameter is set for future kernels
ensure_future_kernels() {
    local initramfs_hook="/etc/dracut.conf.d/page_poison.conf"

    # Create or update the dracut configuration file if it doesn't exist or doesn't contain the parameter
    if [ ! -f "$initramfs_hook" ] || ! grep -qE "^add_drivers.*page_poison" "$initramfs_hook"; then
        echo "add_drivers+=\"page_poison\"" > "$initramfs_hook"
    fi

    # Regenerate initramfs for all installed kernels
    for kernel in $(rpm -qa --queryformat '%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE}.%{ARCH}\n' kernel); do
        dracut --force /boot/initramfs-$kernel.img $kernel
    done
}

ensure_future_kernels

if ! grep -qE "^\s*$PARAMETER" "/etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-poisoning.conf"; then
    echo "$PARAMETER" | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-kernel-poisoning.conf
fi

# Reload the sysctl settings to apply changes immediately
sysctl -p
```

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure SELinux is installed and enabled
if ! rpm -q selinux-policy-devel &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y selinux-policy-devel
fi

# Set SELinux to enforcing mode
setenforce 1
sed -i 's/^SELINUX=.*/SELINUX=enforcing/' /etc/selinux/config

# Install the necessary packages for AppArmor
if ! rpm -q apparmor &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y apparmor apparmor-utils
fi

# Enable and start the AppArmor service
systemctl enable apparmor
systemctl start apparmor

# Set AppArmor to enforce mode
aa-enforce /etc/apparmor.d/*

# Create a default deny policy for all binaries
echo "profile /bin/* flags=(attach_disconnected) {
  deny { exec } /bin/*
}" > /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.default-deny

# Load the new policy
aa-complain /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.default-deny
aa-enforce /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.default-deny

# Ensure the policy persists across reboots
echo "alias aa-status='aa-status --complain'" >> /etc/profile.d/apparmor.sh
echo "alias aa-disable='aa-disable --complain'" >> /etc/profile.d/apparmor.sh
echo "alias aa-enable='aa-enforce'" >> /etc/profile.d/apparmor.sh

# Reload the profile to apply changes
apparmor_parser -R /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.default-deny

update_ssh_config ClientAliveCountMax "$KEEP_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX"

systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure USBGuard is installed
if ! rpm -q usbguard &> /dev/null; then
    echo "USBGuard is not installed. Please install it first."
    exit 1
fi

# Generate a policy file based on currently connected devices
POLICY_FILE="/etc/usbguard/rules.conf"
TEMP_POLICY_FILE=$(mktemp)

# Allow all currently connected USB devices
usbguard generate-policy --allow-list > "$TEMP_POLICY_FILE"

# Check if the generated policy is empty
if [ ! -s "$TEMP_POLICY_FILE" ]; then
    echo "No USB devices found. The policy file will remain unchanged."
    rm "$TEMP_POLICY_FILE"
    exit 0
fi

# Replace the existing policy with the new one
mv "$TEMP_POLICY_FILE" "$POLICY_FILE"

# Reload USBGuard to apply the new policy
usbguard reload

echo "USB device authorization policy has been updated and applied."

    sed -i "/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=/s/\"$/ $kernel_audit_param\"/" /etc/default/grub
fi

# Update GRUB configuration to apply changes
grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Make the change persistent across reboots by ensuring it's in the kernel parameters
if ! grep -q "$kernel_audit_param" /etc/sysconfig/kernel; then
    echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"$(grep GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX= /etc/default/grub | cut -d'=' -f2)\"" >> /etc/sysconfig/kernel
fi

# Verify that the kernel parameter is set correctly
if ! grub2-editenv list | grep -q "$kernel_audit_param"; then
    echo "Error: Kernel parameter $kernel_audit_param not set correctly. Please check GRUB configuration."
    exit 1
fi

echo "Kernel-level auditing enabled successfully for all kernels and future installations."
```

add_audit_rule_augenrules() {
    if ! grep -q "$audit_rule" /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules; then
        echo "$audit_rule" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/99-logins.rules
        augenrules --load

    fi
}

if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    add_audit_rule_augenrules

[Unit]
Description=Reload audit rules at boot
After=auditd.service

Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/sbin/augenrules --load

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Add the audit rule to /etc/audit/rules.d/privileged_ssh_keysign.rules if it doesn't exist
if ! grep -q "$AUDIT_RULE" /etc/audit/rules.d/privileged_ssh_keysign.rules; then
    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/privileged_ssh_keysign.rules
fi

# Reload the audit rules to apply the new rule
augenrules --load

# Ensure the rule is loaded regardless of which rule-loading mechanism auditd uses
if ! grep -q "privilege_ssh_keysign.rules" /etc/audit/audit.rules; then
    echo "@include /etc/audit/rules.d/privileged_ssh_keysign.rules" >> /etc/audit/audit.rules
fi

# Make the changes persistent across reboots by reloading auditd
systemctl restart auditd
```

# Restart auditd to apply changes
systemctl restart auditd

# Ensure auditd is installed and enabled
if ! rpm -q audit &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y audit
fi

systemctl enable "$AUDIT_SERVICE"
systemctl start "$AUDIT_SERVICE"

# Configure auditd to send email on low disk space
cat << EOF > /etc/audit/plugins/email.conf
action_mail_command = /usr/bin/mailx -s "Audit Log Low Disk Space Alert" $EMAIL_RECIPIENT
EOF

# Ensure the mailx utility is installed for sending emails
if ! rpm -q mailx &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y mailx
fi

# Configure auditd to use the email plugin on low disk space
sed -i "/^space_left_action =.*/c\space_left_action = SYSLOG,EXEC=/etc/audit/plugins/email.conf" /etc/audit/auditd.conf
sed -i "/^action_mail_command.*/c\action_mail_command = /usr/bin/mailx -s \"Audit Log Low Disk Space Alert\" $EMAIL_RECIPIENT" /etc/audit/plugins/email.conf

sed -i "/^admin_space_left_action.*/c\admin_space_left_action = SYSLOG,EXEC=/etc/audit/plugins/email.conf" /etc/audit/auditd.conf
sed -i "/^action_mail_command.*/c\action_mail_command = /usr/bin/mailx -s \"Audit Log Low Disk Space Alert\" $EMAIL_RECIPIENT" /etc/audit/plugins/email.conf

# Restart auditd to apply changes
systemctl restart "$AUDIT_SERVICE"

#!/bin/bash

# Define variables
AUDIT_LOG_DIR="/var/log/audit"
LOW_SPACE_THRESHOLD="25"

# Function to check disk space and trigger notification if below threshold
check_disk_space() {
    local available_space=$(df -h "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | awk 'NR==2 {print $5}' | sed 's/%//')
    if (( available_space <= LOW_SPACE_THRESHOLD )); then
        logger -p daemon.warning "Audit log directory is running low on space: ${available_space}% available"
    fi
}

# Ensure the auditd service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Create a systemd timer to check disk space every hour
cat <<EOF | tee /etc/systemd/system/audit-log-space-check.timer > /dev/null
[Unit]
Description=Check Audit Log Directory Disk Space Every Hour

[Timer]
OnCalendar=*-*-* *:00:00
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
EOF

# Create a systemd service to perform the disk space check
cat <<EOF | tee /etc/systemd/system/audit-log-space-check.service > /dev/null
[Unit]
Description=Audit Log Directory Disk Space Check

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c '/usr/bin/check_disk_space'
EOF

# Reload systemd to recognize the new timer and service
systemctl daemon-reload

# Enable and start the timer
systemctl enable audit-log-space-check.timer
systemctl start audit-log-space-check.timer

# Ensure the check function is available system-wide
echo "function check_disk_space() { local available_space=\$(df -h \"$AUDIT_LOG_DIR\" | awk 'NR==2 {print \$5}' | sed 's/%//'); if (( available_space <= $LOW_SPACE_THRESHOLD )); then logger -p daemon.warning \"Audit log directory is running low on space: \${available_space}% available\"; fi }" | tee /etc/profile.d/audit-log-space-check.sh > /dev/null

# Make the script executable
chmod +x /etc/profile.d/audit-log-space-check.sh
